source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 15.3.1 ACL permissions (pp. 285-291)

questions.q: What problem do ACLs (Access Control Lists) solve?
options: They let you grant permissions to specific extra users/groups beyond the standard owner/group/others model
questions.why: The three ugo identities can't express 'this one extra user gets rx'; ACLs add per-user/per-group permission entries.

questions.q: View the ACL of the directory /project.
questions.why: getfacl displays a file/directory's ACL entries.

questions.q: Grant the user tom read and execute (rx) ACL permission on /project.
questions.why: setfacl -m modifies the ACL; u:tom:rx adds tom with rx (use g: for a group).

questions.q: In getfacl output, what does 'mask::rwx' represent?
options: The maximum EFFECTIVE permissions - a user's real ACL rights are the logical AND of their entry and the mask
The default permissions for new files
The permissions of the root user
questions.why: The mask caps effective permissions; a user granted rwx but with mask r-x effectively gets r-x.

questions.q: What does a trailing '+' in an ls -l permission field (e.g. drwxrwx---+) indicate?
options: The file/directory has ACL entries set
The file has the sticky bit
questions.why: The + shows that extended ACL entries exist beyond the basic permissions.

questions.q: Remove the ACL entry for user tom from /project.
questions.why: setfacl -x removes a specific ACL entry; setfacl -b removes ALL extended ACL entries.

questions.q: Set a DEFAULT ACL on /project so that NEW files inherit rx for user tom.
questions.why: The d: prefix sets a default ACL, applied to files created later (must operate on a directory).

questions.q: What is the difference between a recursive (-R) ACL and a default (d:) ACL?
options: -R applies to files/dirs that ALREADY exist; d: applies to files created in the future
-R is for new files; d: is for existing files
-R only works on files, d: only on links
questions.why: Recursion covers existing contents; the default ACL is what newly created entries inherit.
